domain: Domain 1 - General Security Concepts: Cryptographic Solutions
question: Felicia wants to deploy an encryption solution that will protect files in motion as they are copied between file shares as well as at rest, and also needs it to support granular, per-user security. What type of solution should she select?
moreText:
What the question is asking

This question is asking you to decode the scenario and choose the option that best matches the exact job described. The topic is cryptography and data protection. The exam is not asking which option sounds generally security-related; it is asking which option performs the specific function in the clue.



Key clue

The key clue is: “files in motion as they are copied between file shares as well as at rest, and granular, per-user security”. This is the part of the question that should drive the answer. Read the answer choices through that clue, not through general familiarity with the terms.



Correct answer

The best answer is B. File encryption.

File encryption protects individual files. Because the protection is tied to the file, it can follow the file when it is copied or moved, and it can support more granular user-level access.

This is the best choice because it directly satisfies the clue: “files in motion as they are copied between file shares as well as at rest, and granular, per-user security”. It is the most precise match for what the scenario needs.



Why the other options were not chosen

A. Partition encryption — This was not chosen as the final answer because it answers a different question, is broader/narrower than the clue, or does not provide the specific function requested. Partition encryption protects one partition or volume area. It is useful when only a section of a disk needs protection, but it is not normally the best answer when the question asks for per-file, per-user protection.

B. File encryption — This is the answer to choose. File encryption protects individual files. Because the protection is tied to the file, it can follow the file when it is copied or moved, and it can support more granular user-level access. It is the closest match to the wording of the question.

C. Full-disk encryption — This was not chosen as the final answer because it answers a different question, is broader/narrower than the clue, or does not provide the specific function requested. Full-disk encryption (FDE) protects the whole drive. It mainly protects data if a laptop or drive is lost, stolen, switched off, or locked; once a user is logged in and the drive is unlocked, the system can read the data.

D. Record-level encryption — This was not chosen as the final answer because it answers a different question, is broader/narrower than the clue, or does not provide the specific function requested. Record-level encryption protects individual database records, often with a separate key per record. It is a database-focused idea, not ordinary file-share protection.



Exam rule to remember

Match the clue to the function. If the clue says “files in motion as they are copied between file shares as well as at rest, and granular, per-user security”, choose the option that does that job most directly: File encryption.

domain: Domain 1 - General Security Concepts: Cryptographic Solutions
question: Jacob is concerned that the password used for one of his organization's services is weak, and he wants to make it harder to crack by making it harder to test possible keys during a brute-force attack. What is this technique called?
moreText:
🕵️‍♂️ Step 1: Ditch the Fluff & Decode the Question
If you strip away the story, CompTIA is basically asking you one simple thing here: 'What security trick slows down a hacker who is trying to guess millions of passwords or keys a second?' They love to use fancy phrasing like "harder to test possible keys during a brute-force attack," but all that means in plain English is: Make the hacker's computer work slower.



🔑 Step 2: The Core Concept
The winner is Key Stretching. Think of key stretching like adding a mandatory delay to a safe every time someone enters a wrong code. If a human does it once, they barely notice the delay. But if a hacker's computer is trying to guess 10 million combinations, adding a tiny fraction of a second of extra math to every single guess completely breaks their strategy. It stretches out the time it takes to crack the key from a few hours to decades.



❌ Step 3: Why the Rest Are Wrong (Spotting the Traps)
CompTIA deliberately chose options that start with the word "Key" just to make your eyes blur. Let's look at why they don't fit:

• Master keying: This is a physical lock concept (like a landlord's master key that opens every apartment door). It has absolutely nothing to do with stopping a digital brute-force attack.
• Key rotation: This just means changing your passwords or cryptographic keys every 90 days. It’s a good habit, but it doesn't make a hacker's software run any slower while they are actively trying to guess your current key.
• Passphrase armoring: This isn't even a real Security+ term. They literally made up a cool-sounding phrase to see if you would panic and pick it. Don't fall for the fake vocabulary!



💡 The Exam Hack for Next Time
When you are sitting in the exam and you see the words "brute-force" combined with "make it harder/slower to guess," your brain should instantly look for Key Stretching (or its two specific types: bcrypt and PBKDF2).

domain: Domain 1 - General Security Concepts: Cryptographic Solutions
question: Rick wants to make offline brute-force attacks against his password file very difficult for attackers. Which of the following is not a common technique to make passwords harder to crack?

options.C: Use of a purpose-built password hashing algorithm
options.D: Encrypting password plain text using symmetric encryption

answerText: Encrypting password plain text using symmetric encryption
moreText:
What the question is asking

This is a negative-style question about cryptography and data protection. That means you are not looking for the most obvious normal example. You are looking for the choice that does not belong, is least likely, or is not normally part of the concept being tested. In Security+ questions, this wording is easy to miss, so first identify the normal group, then pick the exception.



Key clue

The key clue is: “not a common technique to make passwords harder to crack”. This is the part of the question that should drive the answer. Read the answer choices through that clue, not through general familiarity with the terms.



Correct answer

The best answer is D. Encrypting password plain text using symmetric encryption.

Symmetric encryption uses the same shared secret key to encrypt and decrypt. It is efficient, but securely sharing the key is a major challenge.

This is the best choice because it is the exception. The question’s wording points to something that is not normally part of the group being tested, and Encrypting password plain text using symmetric encryption is the option that does not fit that group as well as the others.



Why the other options were not chosen

A. Use of a salt — This was not chosen because it fits the normal idea being tested better than the selected exception. A salt is a unique value added to a password before hashing. It prevents identical passwords from having identical hashes and makes precomputed rainbow tables much less useful.

B. Use of a pepper — This was not chosen because it fits the normal idea being tested better than the selected exception. A pepper is a secret value added to password hashing and stored separately from the password database. It can add protection, but it must be carefully protected.

C. Use of a purpose-built password hashing algorithm — This was not chosen because it fits the normal idea being tested better than the selected exception. A purpose-built password hashing algorithm is designed to store passwords safely by being slow and resistant to cracking. Examples include bcrypt, scrypt, PBKDF2, and Argon2.

D. Encrypting password plain text using symmetric encryption — This is the answer to choose. Symmetric encryption uses the same shared secret key to encrypt and decrypt. It is efficient, but securely sharing the key is a major challenge. It is the closest match to the wording of the question.



Exam rule to remember

For negative questions, slow down and ask: “Which option does not belong with the others?” Here, Encrypting password plain text using symmetric encryption is the exception the question is looking for.
